blocks.text: I looked, and there was a white horse! Its rider had a bow; a crown was given to him, and he came out conquering and to conquer.
2 : The symbolizes a conquering power that none can resist; in 19.11–13 the reference is to Jesus. The given to the rider as he is sent forth suggests a guarantee of victory.
When he opened the second seal, I heard the second living creature call out, “Come!”c
And out came another horse, bright red; its rider was permitted to take peace from the earth, so that people would slaughter one another; and he was given a great sword.
4 : The symbolizes war and bloodshed. , effectively annulling the “pax Romana,” the self-congratulatory designation of Roman imperial rule as “the Roman peace.”
When he opened the third seal, I heard the third living creature call out, “Come!”c I looked, and there was a black horse! Its rider held a pair of scales in his hand,
5 : The symbolizes famine, which follows upon war.

and I heard what seemed to be a voice in the midst of the four living creatures saying, “A quart of wheat for a day's pay, and three quarts of barley for a day's pay,e but do not damage the olive oil and the wine!”

6 : , sold at exorbitantly inflated prices because of war and famine (cf. 2 Kings 6.24–25). The prohibition against harming the and the (neither olive trees nor grape vines are newly planted each year) suggests that the famine is limited.
When he opened the fourth seal, I heard the voice of the fourth living creature call out, “Come!”c

I looked and there was a pale green horse! Its rider's name was Death, and Hades followed with him; they were given authority over a fourth of the earth, to kill with sword, famine, and pestilence, and by the wild animals of the earth.
6.1–8: Four seals of destruction.
8 : The symbolizes pestilence and death. , the Greek god of the underworld; cf. 1.18. A part indicates wide but not total devastation. , Ex 9.3; 2 Sam 24.13; Ezek 5.12. , Ezek 6.17; 29.5; 33.27.
When he opened the fifth seal, I saw under the altar the souls of those who had been slaughtered for the word of God and for the testimony they had given;
9 : The of the martyrs are said to be because they were killed for the sake of Jesus (2.10; Mt 24.9; Phil 2.17; 2 Tim 4.6).
they cried out with a loud voice, “Sovereign Lord, holy and true, how long will it be before you judge and avenge our blood on the inhabitants of the earth?”

10 : is a cry for divine vindication pronounced by those who have suffered unbearable oppression (e.g., Pss 6.3; 13.2; 90.13; Zech 1.12–16). , God is the one who takes up the cause of those killed for the sake of justice (Ps 79.10 [LXX]).

They were each given a white robe and told to rest a little longer, until the number would be complete both of their fellow servants and of their brothers and sisters, who were soon to be killed as they themselves had been killed.
6.9–11: The fifth seal describes the appeal for divine justice and retribution by the martyrs (v. 10) and the heavenly response to their plea (v. 11).
When he opened the sixth seal, I looked, and there came a great earthquake; the sun became black as sackcloth, the full moon became like blood,
and the stars of the sky fell to the earth as the fig tree drops its winter fruit when shaken by a gale.
The sky vanished like a scroll rolling itself up, and every mountain and island was removed from its place.
Then the kings of the earth and the magnates and the generals and the rich and the powerful, and everyone, slave and free, hid in the caves and among the rocks of the mountains,
calling to the mountains and rocks, “Fall on us and hide us from the face of the one seated on the throne and from the wrath of the Lamb;
for the great day of their wrath has come, and who is able to stand?”
6.1–17: The opening of the first six seals of the scroll, and the enactment of what is written in it.
6.9–8.1: Three seals of judgment.
6.12–17: The opening of the sixth seal unleashes , and its catastrophic effects on the cosmos and on human society are described.
15–17 : All classes of society seek to escape from the wrath of God (Isa 2.10,19).
17 : , the time of judgment (Joel 2.11).
